metadata.summary: Decision matrix for choosing the right minimum product framework. Nine frameworks (MVP, MLP, MMP, MAC, RAT, MDP, MVA, MFP, SLC) suit different market contexts and buyer types.

content.title: Minimum Product Frameworks
content.summary: A decision matrix for choosing the right "minimum X product" framework for a given market context. Nine frameworks — MVP, MLP, MMP, MAC, RAT, MDP, MVA, MFP, SLC — each suited to a different combination of market uncertainty, competitive density, and buyer type. The core rule: picking the wrong framework wastes validation budget and may guarantee failure in crowded markets where users already have "good enough" alternatives.

why.p: "Just build an MVP" is an underspecified instruction in 2026. In a blue-ocean market with unvalidated demand, MVP is correct. In a red-ocean consumer market, shipping a barely-functional product against polished incumbents drives immediate churn. The framework choice determines whether your first release creates learning or reputation damage.

list.item: Starting a new product and deciding how much to build before first release.
Switching market context (e.g. moving from B2C to B2B) and re-scoping.
Team debate: "is this MVP or MLP?" — use the decision matrix to resolve.
Competitive analysis reveals incumbent products are already polished.
Framework already chosen and validated — don't re-litigate mid-build.
Pure technical feasibility spikes — use MFP by default, no matrix needed.
Internal tooling with a captive user base — minimal standards differ.

section.name: framework-catalogue
section.p: All nine frameworks with acronym, purpose, and when-to-use signal.

rule.statement: Choose the framework before scoping. The choice determines what "minimum" means for your release.
rule.rationale: Each framework optimises for a different success criterion. Defaulting to MVP in a crowded consumer market produces a product users ignore.

list.item: MVP — Minimum Viable Product: test core assumptions. Use when entering a new market with an unvalidated idea.
MLP — Minimum Lovable Product: create emotional connection. Use in crowded markets where differentiation is experience.
MMP — Minimum Marketable Product: generate first revenue. Use in B2B and enterprise sales contexts.
MAC — Minimum Awesome Component: build one killer feature. Use when feature-based differentiation is the strategy.
RAT — Riskiest Assumption Test: validate the biggest risk. Use on high-uncertainty projects before any build.
MDP — Minimum Delightful Product: exceed expectations. Use for premium positioning.
MVA — Minimum Valuable Product: deliver business value to the buyer. Use in enterprise ROI-focused contexts.
MFP — Minimum Functional Product: prove technical feasibility. Use for complex infrastructure or novel tech.
SLC — Simple, Lovable, Complete: balance simplicity and polish. Use for consumer apps needing immediate full-loop value.

rule.statement: In crowded markets, an MVP that is merely "viable" will fail. Users already have "good enough" solutions; you need "awesome" or "lovable" to earn a switch.
rule.rationale: Market density changes the minimum bar. A blue-ocean product can be rough; a red-ocean product must clear an existing quality floor before users will try it.

section.name: decision-matrix
section.p: Market-condition to framework mapping with rationale for each selection.

rule.statement: Blue ocean, unvalidated demand: use MVP or RAT. Validate existence of the problem before investing in experience.
rule.rationale: No competing solutions means users will tolerate rough UX to get any solution at all. Validation cost is low, so run RAT first and MVP second.

rule.statement: Red ocean, many competitors: use MLP or MAC. Users must prefer your product over existing alternatives on day one.
rule.rationale: Switching cost is real. A barely-functional product in a crowded market has no gravitational pull; you need either superior experience (MLP) or a single dominant feature (MAC).

rule.statement: Enterprise B2B: use MMP or MVA. Revenue and clear ROI story are required before procurement will sign.
rule.rationale: Enterprise buyers need a business case. "Viable" and "lovable" are secondary to "provably valuable to the organisation's goals".

rule.statement: Consumer, emotion-driven purchase: use MLP or MDP. The product must feel right on first use to generate word-of-mouth.
rule.rationale: Consumer apps succeed on referral loops. Delight drives sharing; functional-but-bland does not.

rule.statement: Technical uncertainty (novel infrastructure, new integration): use MFP first, then expand. Prove it can work before proving it is loved.
rule.rationale: Investing in UX and delight before technical feasibility is confirmed wastes resources if the technical approach fails.

antipattern.description: Applying MVP to every product regardless of market context.
antipattern.reason: "MVP" as a default ignores market conditions. In competitive markets it produces products that users dismiss before forming a habit.

antipattern.description: Choosing MLP or MDP for an unvalidated problem.
antipattern.reason: Investing in delight before validating demand is expensive misdirection. Polish a product nobody wants is still a product nobody wants.
